summary: Reporter requests a Visual Studio project template or downloadable starter solution for cross-platform SkiaSharp development, citing difficulty getting started and repeated failures over multiple years.

reproEvidence.environmentDetails: Not specified — reporter is asking for project setup guidance, not reporting a runtime failure

repoLinks.description: Maintainer (mattleibow) response: acknowledges the ask, says a template isn't strictly necessary, commits to looking at a tutorial

analysis.summary: Reporter wants a Visual Studio 'File → New' template or a downloadable starter project that wires up a shared SkiaSharp rendering library and per-platform host projects. Basic multi-platform samples already exist in samples/Basic/ (MAUI, WPF, Windows Forms, iOS, Android, etc.), but they are not prominently surfaced as getting-started guidance. The maintainer acknowledged the gap and expressed intent to create a tutorial.
analysis.rationale: This is a feature-request for improved developer onboarding — specifically a VS template and/or prominent getting-started documentation. It is not a bug: SkiaSharp functions correctly once set up. Existing samples/Basic/ already demonstrate the multi-platform pattern, but they are buried in the full repository and not linked from the main README 'Using SkiaSharp' section. The maintainer confirmed interest in a tutorial, making keep-open the right action.

keySignals.text: I'd like to be able to open VS, create new project, and select 'Cross Platform Skia'.
keySignals.interpretation: Explicit request for a Visual Studio dotnet new / VSIX project template.

keySignals.text: Every 6 months or so, for the last several years, I pop in here to see if this one basic setup scenario has been addressed. So far, no.
keySignals.interpretation: Long-standing, recurring pain point — high user-friction for onboarding.

keySignals.text: I will still have a look at a tutorial. A template should not be needed as there is just the package and then add the view.
keySignals.interpretation: Maintainer acknowledges the documentation gap and plans a tutorial; considers a full VS template lower priority.

keySignals.text: A single, easy-to-find 'Getting Started' page that shows the recommended way to create a new cross-platform SkiaSharp solution
keySignals.interpretation: Reporter broadened the ask: documentation discoverability is the highest-impact fix.

codeInvestigation.finding: A comprehensive MAUI sample already exists with CPU (SKCanvasView radial gradient), GPU (SKGLView metaball shader with touch), and Drawing (freehand path) pages — covers the multi-platform pattern the reporter wants, but lives inside the full repo.

Samples README documents Basic samples for many platforms (MAUI, WPF, Android, iOS, etc.) but still references legacy Xamarin.Forms and UWP terminology; not linked from root README 'Using SkiaSharp' section.
'Using SkiaSharp' section shows only nuget install command and EAP feed URL — no link to samples, no getting-started walkthrough, no mention of SKCanvasView. This is the main discoverability gap.

workarounds: Install SkiaSharp.Views.Maui.Controls NuGet package, add UseMauiApp<App>().UseSkiaSharp() in MauiProgram.cs, and add <skia:SKCanvasView PaintSurface="OnPaint" /> in XAML — documented pattern used in samples/Basic/Maui.
Use the existing samples/Basic/Maui sample as a starter: clone the repo, open samples/Basic/Maui/SkiaSharpSample.sln, restore NuGet, and run — this is effectively the starter project being requested.
For non-MAUI platforms: each platform has its own sample under samples/Basic/ (WPF, Windows Forms, Console, Android, iOS, etc.).

resolution.hypothesis: The requested feature (VS template) is not strictly necessary because samples/Basic/Maui already demonstrates the setup, but the discoverability is poor. A tutorial page and README link would address most of the friction.

proposals.title: Add Getting Started guide to README
proposals.description: Add a 'Getting Started' section to the root README.md linking to the MAUI basic sample and providing a 3-step quick start: install NuGet, register UseSkiaSharp(), add SKCanvasView with PaintSurface handler.

proposals.title: Publish dotnet new template via NuGet
proposals.description: Package the samples/Basic/Maui structure as a dotnet new template and publish to NuGet. Users could run 'dotnet new skiasharp-maui' to scaffold a working project.

proposals.title: Write a dedicated getting-started tutorial
proposals.description: Publish a step-by-step tutorial (as a GitHub Discussion or docs page) showing: create a MAUI app, add SkiaSharp packages, wire up UseSkiaSharp(), draw a circle. Link it from README and from GitHub Discussions.

resolution.recommendedProposal: Add Getting Started guide to README
resolution.recommendedReason: Lowest effort, highest immediate impact. The samples already exist — linking and summarizing them costs less than a VS template and unblocks most users. The maintainer has indicated they are open to a tutorial.

actionability.reason: Valid feature request acknowledged by maintainer. Existing Basic samples partially fulfil the ask but the discoverability gap is real. Keeping open to track tutorial/template work.

actions.description: Point reporter to existing Basic/Maui sample and outline the planned tutorial
actions.comment:
Thanks for the detailed feedback — and for coming back repeatedly to check on this. Apologies for the friction.

While a full Visual Studio template isn't available yet, the repository already contains a multi-platform starter sample that covers exactly the use case you're describing:

**samples/Basic/Maui** — includes CPU rendering (`SKCanvasView`), GPU/shader rendering (`SKGLView`), and a freehand drawing canvas, all wired up for Android, iOS, Mac Catalyst, and Windows in a single MAUI solution.

To use it as a starter today:
1. Clone the repo
2. Open `samples/Basic/Maui/SkiaSharpSample.sln`
3. Restore NuGet and run on your target platform

The minimum setup for a new MAUI project is:
```csharp
// MauiProgram.cs
builder.UseMauiApp<App>().UseSkiaSharp();
```
```xml
<!-- YourPage.xaml -->
<skia:SKCanvasView PaintSurface="OnPaint" />
```
```csharp
void OnPaint(object sender, SKPaintSurfaceEventArgs e)
{
    var canvas = e.Surface.Canvas;
    canvas.Clear(SKColors.White);
    canvas.DrawCircle(e.Info.Width / 2f, e.Info.Height / 2f, 100, new SKPaint { Color = SKColors.Red });
}
```

We agree that this should be more discoverable — a proper Getting Started page and/or `dotnet new` template would remove significant friction. Keeping this open to track that work.
